title: Justice subcommittee A
description: This is "Justice subcommittee A" by New Zealand Parliament on Vimeo, the home for high quality videos and the people who love them.

chunks.text: Yes, good morning. Yeah, good morning. Now, we've got 10 minutes for 10. Okay, so we start, right? Yes, please. Okay, so, Kia ora, everybody. My name is Cosmo Geoffrey. I'm currently the President of the Hantemri Howard League for Penal Reform, and with me today is Buster Thompson, our Secretary. As we stated in our written submission, the Howard League has been advocating for penal reform for over 100 years. While we primarily engage in advocating for reform related to prisons, we also pride ourselves on promoting an evidence-based consideration

of all proposed legislation that relates to justice and civil liberty. We are aware that there are other organisations who provided much more detailed submissions on this topic. The Howard League would like to support those organisations in submitting that we impose the needless expansion of police powers. We believe we should be strengthening privacy laws rather than weakening them. More especially, when it was revealed that over 50% of those images taken by the police were persons of Maori and Pacifica descent. As an organisation, the Howard League is confronted daily by the inherent discrimination that is rife within our justice system. Polynesian and Maori

people are totally over-represented in all the cohorts we advocate for the New Zealand justice system. And the police are no exception. More Maori and Pacifica people are stopped, apprehended, arrested, tried in our courts, and imprisoned by a factor of at least three times compared to Pacifica subjects. Considering the disproportionate impact this will have on Maori, we think that more effort is required to work with Maori and Maori organisations in developing legislation related to the collection and storage of information. Consequently, the Howard League urges

the Justice Committee to take a breath and consider fully the implications of this legislation. In preparing our submission, we basically decided that it's easy to criticise, anyone can criticise, but what we want to do is try and provide some answers. So we researched what other European jurisdictions do in basically managing public surveillance and storage of collected data. And what we found was really interesting. Most of the countries in the European Union have signed up, in fact all of them as far as we can work out have signed up to the GDPR. I didn't know what

this was either, but it's the General Data Protection Regulation. So they all sign up to the General Data Protection Regulation and this sets strict regulations which control the surveillance and storage of people in public places. For example, there's a proportionality requirement. Police cannot indiscriminately photograph the entire population. The collection of biometric data is prohibited in principle, but if permitted, if it's strictly necessary, it must be proportionate for investigating specific crimes or maintaining security. The storage and retirement. The EU allows the retention of biometric data of suspects and

convicts, but national laws must set appropriate time limits for the reviewing and the need to store that data. For example, in most European countries, if any of the people whose images are taken and stored do not offend in the next 12 months, that data is deleted. And that's what we believe should happen in New Zealand. We could outline this in much greater detail, but to be truthful, the Parliamentary Committee, the Justice Committee and you guys generally have much more efficient and capable research people who can provide this information. We don't need to reinvent the wheel. There are basically instructions and institutions in place that deal

this sort of thing. Why should we be starting out from scratch and having a carte blanche system where people can be willingly recorded and data stored infinitely? We believe that a body such as the Privacy Commissioner should be set up to oversee this legislation and be responsible for the taking and storage of images in the public. If those people do not offend for the next 12 months, then that data should be deleted. That makes sense. There needs to be a clear purpose for why police are taking photos of someone rather than a vague possible future purpose.

Otherwise, effectively, anyone's photo can be taken by the police indiscriminately and stored forever. We believe this not to be a fair and reasonable situation and we want it to be controlled. That's our oral submission and we could answer any questions if there were any. Hey Bill, thank you very much. Hon Dr Duncan Webb. Kia ora Cosmo and Buster. Thanks for all the work that you do. Look, I guess most people would accept that if a police are investigating a crime or something they know as planned as a crime that surveillance and photographs is probably just ifiable. Do you have any comments on them taking photographs where they are really just

gathering information about the behaviours of people that they know are part of criminal networks rather than investigating a particular crime or proposed crime? Well, our view is that, guys, it should be for a particular purpose in that it should be to investigate a crime rather than just otherwise you get this risk of investigating people who they consider associates but are not actually committing any offences. I think it goes too far to be investigating or taking photos of this biometric information of people who are not actively involved in a crime. Thanks, Bill. Just personally, I don't know if this helps, but it's no secret that I have been in prison. It was over 25 years ago. I was down in Twizel

the other day and I got stopped by the police on my jocati and he asked my license. Within seconds, he brought up my history, started talking about something that happened 25 years ago, and I was left standing there wondering, haven't I done time? Shouldn't I be allowed to get on with my life? And I'm just wary of collecting information on people that can be called up within seconds and used against them when it doesn't relate to the whole idea of taking images of people. All right. Well, thank you for sharing that, particularly that your last personal comment, which was very interesting. And both of you this morning joining us on this submission. So,

thanks for your time. Right, now we're going to go to the news. No other questions I take it? No. Yeah. Thank you. Thank you very much, Cosmo. Right, we're going to go to the Royal Association of New Zealand. And hopefully we've got a couple of people. Jacob Perry's there, I see. And maybe Lloyd Gallagher. If you're free, please join us. Do we have Lloyd? Yeah. Lloyd is there, but he's on mute. All right. Well, that's all right. I can, I can speak on what's half. All right. We've got 10 minutes with you. So please leave a little bit of time for questions. Thank you. Thank you. Good morning. So yes, I'm Jacob Perry, appearing with me as Mr Lloyd Gallagher.

We're appearing on behalf of the Law Association's Technology and Law Committee. And thank you for giving us the opportunity to appear this morning. I won't re-tread the written submissions that we have filed, but this morning I just wanted to touch on three points. Firstly, whether or not this bill, as it is, directly responds to the issue which it sets out to address. I want to make some comments on the scope and threshold provisions in the bill. And then also some general comments about the technological context in which this is occurring. And I suppose at the outset, I just want to be clear that or be clear

that we acknowledge the underlying legislative objective here. So the Supreme Court decision in Tamifuna and before that the IPCA, Privacy Commissioner, Joint Inquiry, there is uncertainty arising out of that. And there is a need for a clear basis for information collection. What we want to ensure is that in addressing this issue, we don't end up with either further uncertainty or finding that we address the issue in a way that is inconsistent with individuals' reasonable expectations of privacy. So the first point I wanted to make was just linking this back to where we've come from with the Supreme Court decision in

Tamifuna. Now that decision didn't simply say police can't take photos in public. It turns on a lot more than that. There's taking the image, identifying the person, retaining the image for intelligence use without any defined purpose and without any framework behind it for retention or oversight, destruction, et cetera. The cabinet paper which preceded this bill specifically referenced the judgment of Justice Koch in Tamifuna. And in that judgment, His Honour said that what was needed was obviously some sort of statutory authority for this, but also a clear framework for retention,

use and destruction. And there was a comment that the limits and the broad framework for any such database should be determined by Parliament by amendment of the Policing Act. So the bill that we have now inserts collection powers, but it doesn't do those other things. It doesn't give us a framework after that for retention and use and destruction. And that's the really, really important part here, which I think with the bill is a structural problem. The importance when it comes to privacy rights, the importance is the use. I think initially in relation to this bill, it was anticipated that we would account for this by including some sort of regulation making power, but that doesn't seem to have carried through to

the bill itself. And I'm not 100% sure why that is, but that could have been one of the ways in which this was addressed. So that's point number one from our submissions is that some thought needs to go into what the framework after collection of data looks like and how that is going to be implemented. So if there's authority to collect it, what restrictions are there going to be on its further use? The second point that I wanted to address, which I think is set out in more detail in our written submissions, but just the threshold and scope of collection powers that we're dealing with here are broad. So we have section 45a,

which gives us our purposes, some specified purposes, but also any other lawful purpose connected with functions or activities of the police. Now, when you pair that with the threshold in section 45d, because this purpose is open-ended, our concern is that it leads to a risk of indiscriminate collection without a clearly defined purpose, which is the same concern that was being echoed in Timofuna and before that the joint inquiry. 45d, which I just referenced, sets the threshold for intelligence collection and at the moment it is or it could be used

in a vague way. So the information will or may support police functions or activities. So that just on its face that could support or sorry, could permit collection on a purely speculative basis. So well, this might be useful in the future, but I don't have any real reason to collect it right now. So there's a threshold issue in our view. And the third point I wanted to make on the provisions was just about continuous recording of sound and video solely for intelligence purposes. Now in the bill, this is subject to a reasonableness test, but I do note that in the

cabinet paper that continuous recording for intelligence purposes was not intended to be included. And it's unclear why the bill has, I suppose, taken a different direction. So our submission, well on that at least, if there is going to be an authority for continuous intelligence only recording, then needs to be a framework around that. So what sort of duration medium is anticipated? Who can authorize this? Is this going to be going to be reviewed? Can it be how long can it be held? These sorts of things. I realize I'm whipping through my time here, but

so the third point that I'll make is I think more of a general one, which has to do with the technological context in which all of this is occurring. So not just police technology or technology in New Zealand and globally at the moment is enabling a greater use of mass data and it's moving at quite a fast pace. If we are introducing broad collection powers without a framework that governs future use of data and retention, the practical effect is we're going to vastly expand the volume of information being put into databases. And we may not currently know or even anticipate the use that it's going to be put to. There was a comment that came

from the Supreme Court in Tamewhuna that advances in technology may transform the practical effect of otherwise familiar police practices. So intelligence notings are familiar, commonplace police activity, but the modern version of that which we seem to be looking at is including much larger massive amounts of personal information being pushed into police databases and the ability to use and analyze it at scale that is not clear to us at present. So that's the overall point being I think how the start is going to be used. Actually I can stop this. We've got Dr Lawrence Sunan got a question for you.

Thanks Chair Kiota, can you hear me? Yes. Cool. Just on the data front I'm really interested to know also if you've done any work around how that data can be shared with agencies overseas. Are you all familiar with Migrant 5? I'm not. I don't know if Lloyd might. Yeah I'll jump in on this one. Going from there can you see potentially data like this being used by other countries? Yeah look we've already seen some of the work that I'm doing with Curtin University and my PhD around AI and the risks of AI as well is we've already seen the risks of

data being able to be collated and compared and then feed between an AI to another AI and simply data breach risks that are around that. The amount of data that we're looking to collect here and the general risk for public information that is required under different official information acts and all those sort of acts as well as the confidentiality to require some of it to be withheld there is always a general risk of how that data is going to be shared and released in long-term ventures especially with the technology and how it's developing. One of the other risks is with the collaboration that we're now doing with FBI etc internationally we run a risk of that information no longer being able to be within our control. Once it's in the US's hands under

the Patriot Act etc there's a massive amount of risk of that data being put into databases and miscommunicated along different areas. We saw the new AI acts that are coming in that are sort of being floated around America at the moment where one agency is able to provide that information to another agency, Microsoft is able to use it for other purposes under the terms and conditions. It creates a minefield for potential risk and inappropriate use of this information and that's why we think there should be limits on it. Right Honorable Dr Duckham Webb. Thanks for your look. Little less technical than Lawrence but look I'm interested in whether you think in terms of legitimate collection of information there is a tenable way to frame

intelligence gathering rather than investigation because it seems to be one of the things that we're really struggling with. We all accept that gathering data for investigations is one thing but it's this loose kind of concept of being able to go about and collect intelligence which seems to be hard to pin down. Well that's one of the risks that we've always had is that the intelligence side of things is always about the potential for misuse of behavior in the future. So we have a whole security system and securities act around collecting that sort of intelligence. Then we're talking about generally collecting ordinary people's information in the general public. They're not quite in my opinion they're not quite the same thing because what we do for

intelligence collection really has a lot of frameworks around that and a bit more broader approach whereas what we talked about in general public when you're walking down the street is more around the protection of an individual where there is no potential crime that may be one in the future. So this comes back to the same sort of arguments we had with DNA testing in the early years where how long do you keep DNA information? Is it for a potential rape or abuse in the future? And we issued the limits on that information B and rightly pointed out by some others GDPR does put some limitations on that and in my opinion I think we should follow that same sort of approach because it does create that better balance. Jacob did you have anything to add to that? No no I just think that even if we're talking about general intelligence purposes

I think we have to start from a place where we have a framework around you know how long well for what purposes it's going to be used data is going to be used for and that's really the big problem with the bill at the moment is that it's just it's left a big blank space in there and it is a tricky question I acknowledge the question but the answer to that question isn't leaving the answer blank. All right hey guys we've gone over time but very good submission and drew out some points that we haven't heard before so thank you for both of you for coming on this morning. Thank you we're

going to Caroline Young from the Retail New Zealand hopefully Caroline said good morning you've turned up in person and we're not there with you this morning. Hey good hey lovely to see you again looking forward to hearing what you want to say. Thank you very much and just introduce my colleague. I'm Denise Powell and I'm the Epixie Minister of Retail NZ. Thank you very much for the time to chat this morning we in principle support the bill. What there is a small exception as has been heard in the previous two submissions what we'd say in terms of retailers that the majority of New Zealanders would be walking through a retail store at least once a week or once a fortnight across New Zealand. We have a high degree of recidivist offending in retail where

people are coming back again and again and again and we know that retail crime is a significant problem. We want to ensure that police have the right tools in order to be able to capture offenders and then obviously send them through the justice system in a way in which they'll get charged and in a way that will be meaningful. But we do agree with the prior two submissions that you know we need to ensure that there's the right controls that are sitting in place for you know the framework of any collection of data. We sit here believing that the police do a really great job and that they are collecting information for the right reasons. We would start at a not at the lowest common denominator in a space that's starting to say

on the position that we believe they do the right work. We need to give them the right tools that needs to be the right framework and have the right protections including you know they need to comply to privacy as much as anybody else and proportionality also needs to comply. You know and we would think smarter people than us would be able to look into what's overseas that we could learn from you know they did talk about the general data protection regulation in Europe you know maybe that's something that someone could look into but but as long as we've got the right framework and protections to ensure that people are protected we believe that police need the tools to do the job they would be the key things for our perspective. And what about the expanding the road closures you're happy with all that? The road closures yes the expansion of the road closure powers we think

again police need to be able to have the right tools in place to be able to keep our communities safe from harm and whether that is in terms of being able to give out fines and have the correct penalties in place to ensure that police can close off roads when required and stop people from from going into those areas when it is not a safe environment for them to be to be in we're we're generally supportive again of that. I'll just add just in a retail environment you know we're seeing guns presented at store these days so it's not just machetes and knives and and other weapons but we're seeing people turn up with guns and when those situations happen you

know we get quick response from police they need to be able to call knife areas and make sure that no one else comes into those public spaces because retail environments are a sort of a pseudo public space where you've generally got free entry into so having those controls to close off areas to ensure that our communities are safe are really important. All right good right members questions. Just in terms of your most retailers in terms of this storage I see you support that I see that you support police being able to record in private premises such as a retail store so I presume that's what you mean by that. What's the norm for retailers in terms of collecting

images particularly video images in a shop or whatever what is there a norm around that? I would say 99 percent of retailers have CCTV and there's you know wide use of CCTV CCTV across retail you know multiples cameras throughout store people should be checking those things and they're compliant to legislation around how long they need to hold on how long they can hold on to that information for and police you know being able to come back into store I for instance happen to be able to review tape is important it's something like a 30 or 60 day something that I don't actually have that time on the tip of my tongue I'm sorry but it is a much longer period of time than say for example facial recognition where you know if a retailer

is using that it's deleted within a minute hope it being collected. Okay right Camilla, Village? Yeah just to follow up supplementary to the chair's question I just wanted to know with the obviously I understand the vast majority of retailers will be very compliant with providing the CCTV footage but in those instances where that isn't available in the in the store or for some reason not made available this bill may allow the ongoing surveillance outside a retail store even if that is considered a private place that's visible publicly and obviously with retail stores that might be more likely to be visible publicly than in other private places and so is that

something that you've consulted any of your members on in terms of your support for that ongoing surveillance or is that is it more of the provision of CCTV so you wouldn't imagine that would be utilized in many instances? There's probably two answers to that question the first piece I'll go with in terms of member feedback look we've had members come to us and we had a with Privacy Commission and with the police commissioner and have asked them can we share or can police share their watch list or some of their people that they're most worried about with the retailers watch lists if they've got FRT so that we can make sure we can keep our stores safe so some retailers

are really open about being able to have police insert data into their space obviously at the moment that you can't do that but I would also expect as a second point would be that we would if police are wanting to put in place I guess you'd call it surveillance for want of a better word we would expect that there would be adequate reasoning that sits in behind that and purpose for it so it's not just a we're not just surveilling the whole country for you know for anything but there's actually specific reasons to understand why someone would be coming to that space on a regular basis and is undertaking something that would be viewed to be illegal and will lead them to prosecution down the line so there would have to be something substantive to it. All right hey thank you Caroline and Denise for your presentation nice to see you again.

Nice to see you. Thanks. Right now we've got Anjum Raiman who I see you are there. As-Salaamu Alaykum. Good morning. We've got five minutes with you. Welcome Salam. I'm presenting as not as an expert but just as a member of the public and in my written submission I talked about the oversurbalance of our communities which was effect up to at least May 2018 per the Royal Commission of Inquiry report on the Christchurch Mosque terrorist attacks and you know in this instance and for some of the powers in this bill what we're looking at is young Maori men who were the target of over surveillance

and over surveillance is part of over securitisation and is not the most effective way to reduce crime. We saw after the Christchurch Mosque attacks a securitisation of mosques with surveillance cameras and various other measures in place but I don't believe that is what would make the mosque safer if anyone was so inclined they'd just kill people as they came out onto the street. Do we then securitise the whole society? In the US if we take that instance as an example where they have so much securitisation of shops you know glass brunts you can't interact with people directly the things that are happening in schools for our kids their kids sorry having to do drills

and all these kinds of things but it hasn't really you know resulted in lower gun related deaths so what I'm saying is that prevention efforts are much more effective and better worth our resources and I do want to acknowledge the support of the New Zealand police after the Christchurch Mosque attacks and that my submissions are presented with the utmost respect for that support and on the same theme of over surveillance and securitisation this bill potentially removes the right to political protest and the law commission have laid out their concerns in this regard and if we look at what is happening overseas our concerns or my concerns are that

this kind of thing could start to happen here so for example in the UK we're seeing the criminalisation of elderly pacifists through the use of terrorism laws and incidences where there's no lies we're lost we've seen over overriding of basic protection so for example some of those people that have been tried around damaging military assets the jury that has been making decisions are not allowed to know that if they convert them or if they find them guilty that they will be sentenced under terrorism laws and not just laws related to vandalism of property

I mean this kind of overreach is incredibly scary and that's what slippery slopes are about when we look at what's happening across the ditch in Australia and the banning of one of the states of the term global and to father and we're not seeing the defenders of free speech coming out protecting against the criminalisation of a term of protest against theft of land loss of life access to health education and more within our context yes there's a particular religious group that comes out to harass other religious communities and other communities that protest who misuse the Haka they always target minority groups and not the majority

and I want to offer my solidarity to the Sikh community who are the latest targets the answer is not to change the law in a way that could stifle political protest against state terrorism against the killing of tens of thousands of children against illegal wars when there was never an imminent threat and I could be talking about the illegal invasion of Iraq more than 20 years ago the illegal bombing of Iran and citizens and school girls this year discrimination and others previous speakers have talked had talked about automated decision-making AI algorithms machine learning and as AI is increasingly used in state agencies there is not enough scrutiny and there is no effective regulation of how that data is going

to be used and the ongoing effect but I had more to say but I think other speakers had come that much better than I could and so the powers in this bill are going to make that worse and in the space and around AI and platform regulation there is a lack of effective regulation overall as it is and what protections there are and now starting to be dismantled and we see that with the BSA being taken away but even if there was no discrimination the collection and holding of data by the state in this manner was so little safeguard is an overreach and you know investing in adequate food secure housing and warm housing education health social cohesion

all of that would solve these problems so much more effectively than what is proposed in this bill thank you all right hey thank you for your submission we're well over time but I didn't want to interrupt you um because I thought what you had to say was very important but I'm pushing more have to move on but thank you for coming on this morning and making your thoughts known to the committee right now we're going to uh professor uh goodness carer I think I saw you uh on if you'd like to join us that would be fantastic we've got uh 10 minutes with you professor are you available

I think you've got your mute on there we are good good morning professor yes good morning uh Mr chair thank you for the opportunity to to present on this in a personal capacity um I just want to clarify that um my role I'm an academic at the University of Auckland I have for decades been researching and teaching and publishing in privacy law um but I'm also a past chair and currently the co-convener of the privacy foundations surveillance sorry I'll just start my video I just realized apologies for that

yes so um just um I'm the co-convener of the privacy foundations surveillance working group however I'm not speaking to you this morning in that role I think the foundation is making a submission on its own account so I'm speaking purely as a as a privacy observer and commentator and academic um and I um for the record I completely support the privacy foundations position we have completely opposed the bill we've taken a in principle view that we oppose the bill and that is also my my position um however I want to address um and I oppose both part one and part two but I want to speak specifically to some specifics of part one on the basis that you know if it does

go ahead then I would like to see some specific safeguards and these relate to things like clarifying the relationship they're largely of a technical nature clarifying the relationship between the bill the amendment bill and the privacy act and the biometric processing privacy code but I want to start this morning by saying by dispelling the notion there's a public you know that people might have the idea that somehow if the police take pictures in public places and even on private property it's the same as you or me you're taking a photo of members of the public there's a huge difference between you or you or I taking a photo and the police because the police then retain that picture that photo that image goes into a database and it can be used in a way

perhaps years later or months or years later that has serious effects on the freedom of the individual concern whereas if you and I take a photo of somebody you know we don't have the power to later on go and arrest them or or detain them or prevent them getting a job if there's a police vetting inquiry so these these are the kinds of distinctions that I think need to be made there's also the concern of what I see is mission creep and I think some of your previous speakers have hinted at this I have also heard rumors about the migration five and I'm currently working in fact I have written a piece on this happy to make that available to the committee on the the proposal by the current consultation with the board of protection authorities with Homeland Security in the United States where to get our visa waiver status

the United States is insisting on access direct access to other countries law enforcement databases and and that is to me very concerning I see there's a question no no you finished yeah so yeah so yeah so that's so that is a concern and and we have examples previously of of American companies like Clearview once they get access to these images they go in public databases and it's very difficult to then you know get get them deleted I want to speak very quickly about the difference between biometric information which this bill authorizes and other forms of biographical information there is a big difference in the age of AI and in the age of facial recognition images are very different from a police

constable taking a note in his or her notebook images can identify people instantly be matched be stored in databases and and and have a much more significant potential you know your face is your face you cannot really get a new face if somebody hacks into it and there's no guarantee for the police or anybody for that matter that these images won't end up for example on on the dark web or eventually you know there's a hack or something like that but I want to also talk about the the over wide definitions such as policing intelligence intelligence or other lawful purposes integrity of policing these purposes are currently defined very broadly in section nine of the act and they are so broad

as to potentially include any of these kinds of use that the police find for the information so that's the mission creek that I'm talking about and you know and when you talk about community support what which is one of the listed purposes in section nine it could be counterproductive because if you're surveilling a target population to try and intimidate them for example rangatahi or something like that you might think well you know we can intimidate people by taking photographs of them deterring them from committing crime that could actually be counterproductive because you know community support really means taking the community with you not necessarily making them the objects of surveillance so that was an issue the other issue I wanted to raise is to dispel the notion that somehow all we're doing is restoring the status quo

the current the I've listed these points in my submission there are sections of the policing act at the moment section of 32 33 and 34 of the principal act which allow the police to retain images and other biographical data and there are various conditions on that that they need to that that information is for a proceed current proceedings or when someone is detained but there's an obligation where the proceeding doesn't carry carry on for the images to be to be deleted so that that's that's that is the status quo and this bill changes that status quo by now allowing those images to be retained potentially indefinitely as I think one of your previous speakers also mentioned there was the issue identified by the independent police

complaints authority and the OPC joint investigation about access and correction to those records and the police previously have struggled to actually I know they have made some improvements and I have a great deal of respect for the police you know they have been trying hard to to improve their data collection and storage practices but we know that there's a there's a possibility that you know these images could be dispersed and it might be difficult to retrieve them and once things go in the national intelligence database I've given an example of cases where you know decades later it comes back to haunt a respectable member of the community when you know there's a police vetting request and somehow their image or their information pops up so the clarity is needed as to the whether the privacy act and the biometric

processing code continues to apply I think they should we have no right of deletion in New Zealand and that is a great concern to me as a privacy academic I think we need our law needs to have retention limits and that's something that's currently not in the law I also want to address section 45c clause that are rather close 45c of the bill which allows police to take images on private property it needs to they need to comply with a reasonableness test but that's not very clear as to how that compares with the more nuanced balancing that's required for example in the biometric privacy processing code which does require a risk assessment to be balanced to get privacy risks to be balanced with the benefits in a more nuanced kind of manner a more finely

grained kind of way of doing that the reasonableness test is just too blunt and in conclusion I also think if this bill goes ahead there needs to be some provision for not only a very very clear statements of the privacy acts acts needs to completely continue to apply but also the need for subsequent powers of audit for the privacy commissioner or the or the independent police complaints authority to be able to audit how these new collection powers are being used and that they're not being used in an overbroad way that that's pretty much all I had to say I'm happy to take questions at this time okay we've got one oh I think we have one from Rima Nakley thanks Mr Chair um thank you sir uh I want to ask one of your earlier references

was about the huge difference between police using pictures taking pictures and members of the public well members of the public taking pictures and um with respect to this uh huge difference um are you am I to understand that uh you would also like to see maybe in the future some changes with respect to laws and how people can take members of the public can take pictures seeing that you um have studied this and you're an expert in this field would you make yeah sorry yeah that's an excellent question it's an excellent question and it's probably beyond the the brief of the current inquiry I guess but um if I could just answer that briefly I think that the office of the privacy commissioner does have very

detailed guidance to two members of the public as to as to their taking of pictures so I think in the first instance members of the public who want to take pictures in public generally our law is pretty relaxed in that respect in New Zealand um it's it's really and there are broad exceptions for example for individuals in the privacy act where they're doing it for their personal or family needs um but if they're doing it in a business context or even a hobby perhaps then apart from the polite convention that you always get the permission of the person you're taking a photo of um there are requirements in the privacy act because pictures are personal information as covered by the act um so that's my brief reply I think you know there's already plenty of guidance around that I'm not sure that we need separate legislation on it I think that the law currently

does allow picture taking and also um addressing some of the points made by earlier earlier speakers today um if a retail members of the retail community who are worried by you know a threat posed by a particular person they are entitled to take photos if there's and then to transfer transmit them to the police the the public the privacy act does have pretty broad exceptions where they have a reasonable basis for believing it's necessary to for follow up to give that information to law enforcement authority but the test is there has to be a reasonable basis you can't just take pictures of everyone and treat everyone as a suspect you know unless you have some reasonable basis for it so professor running in time but I've got two questions um

you'll see that in that case the police the police had in that case made a mistake they had released information that was well out of date it was no longer relevant it was therefore misleading it blighted a very successful career of a person who was a councillor and but at the same time when his lawyers asked for the information police to delete the information from the National Intelligence the Human Rights Committee Human Rights Tribunal was not prepared to do that they said no we can't second guess the police reason for we're retaining that information but we think they should be very careful before releasing it so these are these are with the tensions I think will come if you retain the all of this information down the track it could cause issues to individuals and how are you going to make sure that it's it's you know very

discussion 30 years of personal experience and the wealth of experience of my um committee and also organization where we have spent our lifetimes working on watching decision making that police make watching the surveillance and the monitoring that they engage in and the searches that they conduct and so I come to speak in opposition to this bill not just as a criminal defense lawyer but also as a general lawyer and a citizen of New Zealand because for the last 10 years I have been hoping that one day I would be sitting in the seat talking to a select committee about the need for police to wear body cameras to record their conduct for transparency accountability and to support the rule of law and the effective operation of a safe

and fair criminal justice system and yet I find myself today sitting here arguing about how far the state should be allowed to go in collecting information about ordinary New Zealanders who have done nothing who are not suspected of committing a crime but where the police would be able to collect images sound and other personal information in the hope that they may at some point serve a policing function in the future without any definition this bill represents a significant expansion of police power particularly around intelligence gathering and public area enforcement these changes are being advanced without consultation effectively without safeguards and importantly without

justification it matters because legislation of this kind doesn't just affect people that you might think have committed a crime it affects every New Zealander who walks through every public space attends a protest gathers with friends or interacts on a daily every day basis the bill ostensibly was introduced in response to the Supreme Court decision and Tommy Founa and the Queen the King sorry in that case the court found that the police had acted unlawfully when photographing a member of the public during a routine traffic stop and retained the image without lawful investigation basis our Supreme Court was clear even in public spaces New Zealanders retain

reasonable expectations of privacy the state cannot collect and permanently retain personal information arbitrarily just because the technology exists and rather than responding to a judgment which I know our Minister of Justice has made clear that if there's any legislative discussion by our Supreme Court that he doesn't like he'll simply change the law this bill goes much further it seeks to not only legalize conduct that our Supreme Court has said would be wrong but it substantially expands police authority to retain and collect personal information and it's an extraordinarily broad power it shifts us away from targeted

policing based on suspicion or investigation towards speculative information gathering collecting information today about citizens in case it might be useful tomorrow and that should concern us all because once information enters a state intelligence system experience tells us that it is impossible to control what happens to it how long it's kept how widely it's shared and what purpose it's put to what this bill does is remove an existing requirement under our privacy law that information gathering needs to be necessary for a lawful purpose the change is subtle and word-in but enormous in consequence that means the threshold for collecting

information is now so low as to be non-existent this is not a bill to assist targeted policing this is a bill to introduce routine surveillance of New Zealanders the privacy commissioner has warned that these powers could have a chilling effect on civil rights and freedoms that warning deserves serious attention when people believe they may be photographed monitored and permanently recorded by the state without suspicion of wrongdoing behavior changes that's not hypothetical people become less willing to engage in protest and democracy less willing to associate freely and history tells us that when broad surveillance powers are expanded they

continue to expand and practice over time one of the key concerns that you have heard time and time again before the select committee in this government's term is the rushing of legislation that dramatically changes the fabric of our society without time for consultation or due regard for consultation from experts working in this field key agencies on this bill have also expressed grave concern about the limited evidence of the reason or justification for this intervention in New Zealanders lives constrained policy analysis flawed application of the New Zealand Bill of Rights and total um abject insufficient consultation with Māori and affected communities this is not about questioning the intentions of the police but it would be wrong for us to not sit

in this room and acknowledge that we are still experiencing a fallout of senior police officers demonstrating a gross misjudgment of decision-making it is not that long ago 2018 when the independent police commissioner released a report about police using the land transport act powers to stop and retain information about people citizens attending a private meeting what that demonstrates to us is that where legislation is without detail where legislation doesn't build in safeguards and necessary constraint left to their own devices the New Zealand police on occasion make errors of

judgment that affect New Zealand citizens so good law is not built on trust and good intentions it's built on clear limits accountability transparency and democratic legitimacy this bill fails on every demand that we would have of it transparency is essential when the state is granted extraordinary power this debate is not about whether the police should be able to investigate crime of course they should the public expects and demands the police keep community safe and have effective tools to do so but effective policing and civil liberties are not opposing values they depend on each other public trust is one of the most important tools policing has

this trust is strengthened when powers are necessary proportionate transparent and subject to meaningful oversight to matakaki the defense laws association and the lawyers appearing before you are not saying do nothing we are saying slow down consult properly define powers clearly and ensure that laws that are made reflect modern democratic expectations of privacy and accountability we are not being obstructive we are being responsible we do not want laws that dramatically change the fabric of our society because of a knee jerk rebellion to a supreme court judgment once powers of surveillance and control are normalized they

will not be rolled back so the decisions that the select committee make and the parliament make will define the relationship between the state and the citizens of new zealand for years to come and what we say is that that relationship should be grounded in rights trust and democratic principle Thank you Elizabeth for that I've got one question from Camilla that village thank you thank you thank you very much for your powerful submission and for putting the submission together with your colleagues I just had a question around the the steps that we could take and thank you for your summation which said that we should be more considerate and go slower we've

again it might just be a lawyer thing but we often say the devil is in the detail and this bill is devoid of detail it's essentially kicking for touch down the road how this bill would if made law effectively be rolled out and my concern and the concern of many lawyers that have had the advantage in the time to review this bill is that when those decisions are being made they will not be made with the principles of privacy and the interests of the overall society and justice of New Zealand being brought in so I think that in short it's a tricky one because obviously my position is that this should just be scrapped back to the drawing board and a proper period of consultation there is no urgency with this legislation that it's not

responding to an immediate need to change the current status quo and what the Supreme Court has said and to engage these powers we've essentially urgency and lacking consultation and lacking proper drafting and consultation around the drafting that's going to lead to error and it's going to be difficult to unwind that error going forward and so I do say pause go back to the drafting making sure that there's at least a link between the necessity and working out more steps for accountability record keeping and decision making because currently the bill is devoid. compliance notice under the privacy act in relation to the taking of photographs in the wire apper is the ability to store those in a way that they can be readily retrieved or indeed deleted when they're not needed anymore we have through that compliance notice been seeking to have all unlawfully taken photographs deleted from police systems and that is one area where police have told us and in fact have said publicly that they have been unable to identify those photos due to inadequacies in their current information management systems so yes there are some significant challenges there what we will inevitably see under the proposal set out in the bill is the taking of photographs of people who have been going about their lawful every day

business those photos are going to end up in a police system next to people who have been convicted or accused of some of our most worst crimes it's all together in one database and the ability for that material to be retrieved I think is an open question given the current inadequacies in the way the systems are working at present and just one last question please Mr. Chair um do you think that introducing this legislation is premature given that the required IT infrastructure to do what it is required through privacy is currently not in place so two things I want to say if this bill proceeds as it is as it is and I would certainly hope that if it was the view of the government to proceed with this legislation

that they would consider the the natures of the amendments we've proposed there would need to be a much greater period set aside before it comes into force in terms of commencement to enable police to put in place in necessary systems procedures and infrastructure to enable it to function effectively second though my first best option is that this part of the bill will be not commenced with and that we start a as I said a considered and careful process with a great degree of public consultation as well of course on the sort of intelligence collection regime there will be appropriate for a police force going forward in the modern era with all the digital innovation available to us okay thank you thank you very much I've got

Todd Stephenson we've got a number of questions coming up with him thank you thank you commissioner and your team for your detailed submission great work a couple of things I want to explore one is I would certainly can't speak for the whole committee but I'd certainly be interested in your additional safeguards that you would like to see put in place I think one of the issues and everything you've raised super is important super important I think we yeah there might be some things we can do to this bill one to address some of the issues you have done and then I really am interested in this this idea around a framework for police intelligence gathering do you think there's a way we could put strengths in the bill by putting in some safeguards but then also

you know noting and say regulations that there has to be this this framework that you're talking about it do you know could you see some somewhere we could yeah some way we could achieve what you want but not not well delay maybe implementation but at least have it available be done in the future sure through you mr chair I guess if I think about safeguards I I think about four areas first tighter thresholds for police collection of information needing to assess whether it's necessary to collect the information whether the privacy benefits of the collection and propose use are proportionate to the expected benefits for policing functions so tighter thresholds

reflecting the privacy principles there second controls on the storage retention and destruction and secondary use of that information collected under the powers in the bill third accountability and transparency provisions such as requirements for record keeping auditing public recording and publication of policies relating to the exercise of the police's powers and fourth of course oversight of police police compliance with safeguards by an independent body one of our concerns of course with the the provisions is drafted in part one is that they displace the the privacy act and therefore the ability of my office to exercise some prudential oversight of what the police is doing in the space in terms of primary regular

primary legislation or regulation I guess first piece would be useful to have even if it's at a higher level such safeguards specified in legislation and then with the ability for for example for the responsible minister to make regulations to provide some more detail we would expect that regulations making power to not be discretionary that it would be in a a a requirement of the minister to make those regulations through the the correct process with public consultation of course if I think about other agencies that are active in this space in the surveillance space we have of course two security and intelligence agencies which carry out their intrusive functions within a very sophisticated and comprehensive network of statutory provisions and oversight

of what they do and one of my concerns of course is that in the area of surveillance we have to as a nation be careful to avoid what I would call surveillance creep by other state agencies we have two agencies who for special in particular reasons have extraordinary surveillance powers and they are and those are undertaken within a as I say a comprehensive statutory framework to protect the rights and freedoms of New Zealand citizens I would be very wary of similar powers being given to other state agencies without such a similar set of safeguards being in place all right maybe a further tip Todd's question I think it'd be useful if you could supply

some further thoughts on it just on the oversight you also suggested in your report the appointment or oversight by the new inspector general police and that's one recommendation earlier we heard about the general data protection regulation that many international countries adopt can you give me provide a view on whether we need both one and are you clear that the inspector general is the right pick organization to do any oversight and we were to impose something sure my colleagues might wish to say more on that but what I would say is that we have seen developments in other countries on the other side of the world and closer to home in Australia where the the oversight

bodies that look after for example the intelligence agencies as I've just been talking about have actually had their their authority extended to cover the police carrying out such surveillance activities but we of course have had the proposal to have an inspector general of police I think at the end of the day would come to how that the responsibilities roles and powers of that agency are drawn up I will see it as a place where there could be a number of oversight bodies I would hope that the office of the privacy commissioner that would remain one of those with oversight responsibilities as well as an inspector general of police anything further you want to say on that I think just to add to that I think the important priorities to make sure this bill

anticipates oversight so that it includes the safeguards that mean that any oversight body can engage with police activities unfortunately the inspector general of police is not yet come to fruition but if the bill is established in such a way that anticipates oversight then you can deal with the choice of oversight body perhaps further down the track once that inspector general comes on on on stream so you're not specifically proposing inspector general but you're saying that that is one cost possibility or are you saying when it comes to surveillance and we've got intelligence we've got other activities we should be adopting what we have for these

GCIS or SIS type arrangement you got a clear view on that I guess what just to clarify for the committee mr. chair what I'm saying is if we went through for example a different process in the one before the committee today where there was a a considered and careful process to develop the spoke standalone legislative framework for police surveillance activities I would absolutely see a role for a future inspector general of police in relation to that what we would need to be sure about though is that we didn't have a situation where the police had commenced for example carrying out a new regime of surveillance activity without that oversight being in place

right okay get the point um you talked about the objective um for holding data and been very clear about when you're saying the bill doesn't section 45 talks about any other lawful purpose and so you have you got clear proposals around how you've tightened that aspect you and we don't have specific wording but we do think that there needs to be a clear uh threshold uh and justification um a necessity test and some sort of uh test of proportionality of the collection of information by police so at the moment the power that's in

section 45 a the other sections that would be introduced by the bill are very broad ranging and in particular we're concerned that the requirement under the privacy act that you need to uh determine whether the collection of information is necessary would effectively be removed by this act so uh but by this bill sorry so we would like um that kind of threshold to be reintroduced in some way do you think that was intentional to remove that or do you think that's just an intentional consequence in terms of reference to the privacy commission of well the um the materials relating to this bill do indicate a policy intent for the privacy

act to still apply our analysis is that it will be displaced in some respects so it is perhaps possible that uh it's been a misunderstanding or an unintentional consequence but we do think that it would be the legal effect of this bill that in particular that first principle in the privacy act which is central to the whole act would be effectively displaced okay so it would be useful get your thoughts on that um around maintaining that aspect the other bit the one bit that no one sort of picked up on but you do raise in your report is about police employees with something that we've asked um the officials about but they understand around that is where you have a crime scene and you've

got people you might want to bring on specialists what's your view on that or did you simply not understand what that term meant yeah we were simply um asking the question i guess um as to whether there was a rationale behind that because some parts of the policing act uh the powers in those parts are restricted to constables others are more general to police employees so we were just wanting to test whether there was a good rationale given that constables have to go through additional um training and requirements in order to meet the test to become a constable so it's good to hear that the committee has asked officials for advice on that point

do you um you made the coming about intelligence and intelligence purpose need to be better defined do you have a clear view around the difference between intelligence intelligence purpose and surveillance as which relates to the act or sorry of the bill yes yes mr. chair that is why we raise the question a lot could be packed into that word the police do have existing guidance documents that refer to their intelligence function and so as part of our discussions prior to the bill being introduced we raised the question about the usefulness of having a definition of intelligence in the bill the clearer the bill is the clearer

New Zealanders be aware of their their rights and privileges in relation to it and a lack of a definition in that space does create more uncertainty we believe and and for not just members of the public but also for police officers and carrying out their job and would you link that to a common definition with what the secret service agencies do or do you think that police have their own form of intelligence gathering that's sort of a different intent obviously you have different purpose one is international or different reason for it do you have a view on that that would be something that the police would need to work through themselves for their own purposes i think and and work up some proposals for our potential legislative amendment to the bill okay one more question on then i'll go to camilla um we've also heard and submissions

earlier today around the need or the sorry the right to have um videos or whatever information that's been collected maybe under surveillance to be deleted uh there's doesn't seem to be any rules perhaps it's going to come in regulation do you have a view around whether there should be a mandatory requirement or you have a right to be able to see what's on file obviously that then becomes subject to criminals actually trying to stop something held but how would you see the balance of that that right to delete material that isn't relevant versus the ability of keeping these in at the safe well mr chair and under the current privacy act provisions everybody has through information privacy principle nine the ability to ask why information is

being retained there is a retention provision which says that information shouldn't be kept for longer than than it's needed or there's a reasonable reason to keep it i think that one of the challenges with the provisions in part one is that as i say it displaces the effects of the privacy act it also displaces that principle there are good reasons not to retain information you don't need one obviously the rights of new zealanders second more information you retain the costly it is to keep that information and third the more information you hold as we've seen with a number of privacy breaches and both the public and private sector the more risk there is that that information could be taken in a cyber attack so there is strong public policy reasons

for having a retention in erasure principle being applying to to information committed in the space chose anything we want to say on rights to erasure in that space just to yeah affirm what you're saying commissioner that the right to deletion and and the principle about accurate and up-to-date information are important data protection principles so while the framework needs to enable police to retain what it needs to retain the framework also needs to have flexibility just to discard the information particularly under a very broad authorization there needs to be an ability

to discard the information that police do not need to retain so that the intelligence being retained is useful and fruitful intelligence not simply scooping up a lot of information that turns out not in fact to be particularly useful and is information about New Zealand citizens and and visitors okay cool right come in thank you and thank you very much for your substantive submission and for your discussion with the committee I just had two questions and just wanting to really understand your submission to us the first is is it your submission that there needs to be a law change following the tamifuna case and the second

question is you've talked about the risk of possible legal challenges if the law is to proceed as it is currently outlined in this bill is it your view that there is an increased risk with this legislation or that simply it remains unclear because of the lack of detail which has been traversed a bit already today thank you I'll start with the second first the risk of possible legal challenges I think I said in my opening remarks that you can't very easily legislate away the power of the court system that is the purpose of the court system after all to interpret the law in relation to certain

facts situations Joe is anything more you want to say on that particular point um I think that the our submission and our submission reflects some of the underlying messages from the joint OPC IPCA report and the tamifuna decision which is to say that the bill really only deals with half the problem it deals with the point of collection of information by police but the messages from the joint report and from tamifuna we're really saying that you have to consider intelligence gathering within the full life cycle of the use of that information and its retention and it's in this this increasingly

intensified technological progress where that information is going into data network databases for analysis and insight and so on so really the message from the tamifuna court and if you think about what his honor justice coach was saying he was really inviting parliament to create a clear framework not just about the authorization for police but about retention use and destruction of information and so our perspective is that without addressing that side of the equation um which is useful for the system it's useful for police to provide greater certainty without that part of the equation the safeguards there's likely still

to be scope for the for information gathering to be challenged within the courts and for the courts to have a look at that under the um the privacy settings which are embedded legally within the privacy act the search and surveillance act and the New Zealand Bill of Rights Act all right hey we have to bring it to a close um but thank you for your submission a weighty submission there's obviously Todd asked some specific stuff and I've asked for some other stuff we'd appreciate if you were able to help the committee us do the deliberations around those aspects so if you could come back to us that'd be very helpful but I want to thank you all for coming through this morning and um giving you all thoughts thank you mr. chair thank you

members uh yes if there are any further uh questions uh very happy uh to respond to them through your advisors to the committee and and we haven't covered everything that was asked by miss billy all right thank you now members we're going to go into private um session just briefly if you want to transfer it thank you everyone
